desc: Air source heat pump funded by Green Communities program installed, reducing GHG 70%.
more_text:
Gloucester was among the initial cohort of communities that achieved Green Community Designation in 2010 with the intent to become a clean energy leader and a better place to live, work, and play. To become a Green Community, the city adopted progressive guidelines and standards including an Energy Reduction Plan, Fuel Efficient Energy Policy, and Stretch Energy Code. As a Green Community, Gloucester has been awarded grants that help fund various energy conservation projects. The air source heat pump installed here reduces greenhouse gas emissions by 3.5 tons annually. In addition, three heat pumps installed at three fire stations where their combined benefit reduces greenhouse gas emissions by 18 tons annually.

The Green Community Designation and Grant Program provides a road map along with financial and technical support to municipalities that pledge to cut municipal energy use by an ambitious and achievable goal of 20 percent over 5 years. To date, the city has invested over 1 million dollars into energy efficiency updates at municipal facilities, including lighting upgrades at several municipal facilities, Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning (HVAC) system upgrades, and a citywide Light Emitting Diode (LED) streetlight retrofit.

In urban settings along coastlines around the world, rising seas threaten infrastructure necessary for local jobs and regional industries. Roads, bridges, subways, water supplies, oil and gas wells, power plants, sewage treatment plants, landfills are all at risk from sea level rise. "By the end of the century, global mean sea level is likely to rise at least one foot (0.3 meters) above 2000 levels, even if greenhouse gas emissions follow a relatively low pathway in coming decades." — NOAA

Nuisance flooding is minor, recurrent flooding that takes place at high tide. Because of sea level rise, nuisance flooding has become a "sunny day" event — not necessarily linked to storms or heavy rain. During nuisance flooding, waves may overtake old seawalls, water may inundate low-lying roads, and storm-water drainage can be diminished. These impacts may not be life threatening, but they disrupt transportation, damage infrastructure, and strain city and county maintenance budgets.
note:
Press release https://gloucester-ma.gov/DocumentCenter/View/10048/02022024---Press-Release---Green-Communities-Grant-Award

Green Communities https://www.mass.gov/guides/becoming-a-designated-green-community

CARP https://gloucester-ma.gov/DocumentCenter/View/8803/5-2022-Gloucester-Climate-Action-and-Resilience-Plan-CARP-Buildings-_10-27-2022?bidId=

desc: Celebrating its 410th anniversary, city completes planting program of 1,000 new trees.
more_text:
In a changing climate, Gloucester's natural resources serve both as a buffer and a refuge, by mitigating climate impacts (such as heat waves and urban heat island effects) and helping the City adapt through nature-based solutions (1). A hot day in Gloucester is considered to be any day above a "feels like" temperature of 89ºF. In 2025 Gloucester was experiencing 7 hot days annually. Due to climate change, this increased to 16 days above 89ºF in 2055 (2).

Trees help slow and even mitigate the impacts of climate change by cooling the streets, absorbing carbon dioxide, providing oxygen, reducing stormwater runoff, and providing habitats for wildlife (4). Trees have proven social and human health impacts by improving mental health of people living around them, promoting physical activity, and building community connections. 400 Trees Gloucester, initiated by Biomimicry New England, began as part of the 2023 quadricentennial celebrations in collaboration with the City of Gloucester. In addition to the beauty trees bring, a neighborhood with trees can be 10° cooler than one without. Trees are a critical source for life, providing the oxygen we breathe, absorbing significant amounts of carbon which lessens the effects of climate change, as well as contributing to the ecosystem. Recognizing that there was greater need than a short-term project could address, 400 Trees Gloucester became a permanent program with a mission to plant and maintain trees throughout Gloucester and to educate youth and adults about how native trees that are climate resilient are critical to our health and local ecosystem (3).
note:
1) Gloucester CARP https://gloucester-ma.gov/DocumentCenter/View/8805/7-2022-Gloucester-Climate-Action-and-Resilience-Plan-CARP-Natural-Resources_10-27-2022?bidId=

2) Heat risk https://firststreet.org/city/gloucester-ma/2526150_fsid/heat

3) 400 Trees https://www.400treesma.org/

4) Environmental impact of trees https://www.arborday.org/environmental-impact#:~:text=As%20trees%20grow%2C%20they%20help,benefits%20to%20us%2C%20every%20day.

desc: Flood barriers installed to further protect Harbor from sea level rise and storm surge.
more_text:
It was understood that, even as early as 2030, a 100-year flood event could inundate many of the city's sewer pump stations, reach Gloucester High School (also an Emergency Dispensing Center), and threaten the City's water pollution control facility. Flood barriers were identified as the critical next step to build on earlier resilience measures. The harbor, as the city's economic center, is especially vulnerable. Many Massachusetts jobs—particularly in fishing, tourism, and beach-related industries—depend on a healthy, functioning coast. As sea levels rise and flooding becomes more frequent, these livelihoods are increasingly at risk, with disproportionate impacts on the coastal residents living below the poverty line. Investments in flood protection reduce the need for repeated repairs after coastal storms, allowing more public funds to remain available for schools, road maintenance, and essential services.

The commercial fishing industry generates roughly $20 billion in sales across New England, yet warming ocean temperatures continue to make historic catch levels harder to achieve. Species such as cod and lobster have shifted northward or farther offshore in response to rising water temperatures. At the same time, increased dissolved carbon dioxide from human activities—primarily the burning of fossil fuels—is changing the chemistry of seawater, making it more acidic. This ocean acidification affects marine species differently, particularly those that rely on calcium carbonate to build shells and skeletons. To address declining or shifting fish stocks, regulators have tightened catch limits, but these measures have also made it more challenging for fishing to remain a sustainable livelihood. The resulting strain on the fishing sector, in turn, reverberates through the tourism economy that is so vital to coastal communities.
note:
Vulnerability https://www.mass.gov/info-details/sea-level-rise?_gl=1*1x6vf1k*_ga*MTc3OTE2NjEzMi4xNzQ0NTcwMTUw*_ga_MCLPEGW7WM*czE3NjQwODI2MjckbzUkZzEkdDE3NjQwODI2MzckajUwJGwwJGgw#who-is-vulnerable-to-sea-level-rise
